roadmap.title: 45-Day Data Science Roadmap
roadmap.description: A rigorous 45-day intermediate curriculum for practitioners who already know Python, basic statistics, and core ML - advancing into production-grade engineering, advanced statistics and causal inference, modern ML and deep learning, MLOps, big data, and a deployed end-to-end capstone.
roadmap.outcome: Ability to engineer scalable data workflows, design and analyze experiments, build and optimize advanced ML and deep learning models, deploy and monitor them in production, and deliver rigorous, business-driving data science.

days.title: Advanced Python for Data Science
objectives: Write clean, idiomatic, and performant Python
Use OOP, decorators, generators, and type hints effectively
topics: Generators and iterators for memory efficiency
days.intermediate_explanation: At the intermediate level you stop writing throwaway scripts and start writing Python that other people (and production systems) can rely on. Object-oriented programming lets you model concepts as classes with encapsulated state and behavior, and dataclasses remove boilerplate for data-holding objects. Decorators are functions that wrap other functions to add behavior (timing, caching, logging) without changing their code, and context managers (the 'with' statement) guarantee setup/teardown like closing files or database connections. Generators and iterators produce values lazily, one at a time, which is essential for processing datasets too large to fit in memory. Type hints, checked by tools like mypy, document expected types and catch a whole class of bugs before runtime, making large codebases maintainable. Mastering functools (caching, partial application) and itertools (efficient iteration) lets you express complex data transformations concisely. These skills separate a data scientist who can only prototype in notebooks from one who can ship robust, reusable code that scales.
days.practical_task: Refactor a procedural data-processing script into a class-based module with type hints, use a decorator to time functions, and rewrite one data-loading step as a generator to reduce memory use.
days.mini_project: Build a small reusable data science utility package (with classes, decorators, generators, and full type hints) that you can import across projects, and run mypy to verify it type-checks cleanly.
days.assignment: Implement a caching decorator and a context manager for a database connection, then write a short note explaining when each pattern is appropriate.

quiz.question: What is the main advantage of a generator over returning a list?
options: It is always faster to write
It produces values lazily, using far less memory
quiz.answer: It produces values lazily, using far less memory

quiz.question: What does a decorator do?
options: Wraps a function to add behavior without changing its code
Converts a class to a dict
quiz.answer: Wraps a function to add behavior without changing its code

days.title: Software Engineering and Testing for Data Science
objectives: Apply software engineering practices to DS code
Write tests for data and models
topics: Testing data and ML code (fixtures, mocking)
days.intermediate_explanation: Data science code that reaches production must be tested and maintainable, not just correct once in a notebook. Software engineering practices bring discipline: organizing code into modules with clear responsibilities, separating configuration from logic, and keeping notebooks for exploration but scripts/packages for anything reused. Testing is central. Unit tests with pytest verify that individual functions behave as expected, and for data science you also write tests for data (schema, ranges, no unexpected nulls) and for model behavior (does accuracy stay above a threshold, does the pipeline run end to end). Fixtures provide reusable test setup and mocking lets you test code that depends on external services without calling them. Linters and formatters like ruff and black automatically enforce style and catch errors, keeping a team's code consistent. Proper dependency management with tools like uv or poetry pins exact versions so your project is reproducible anywhere. Adopting these practices dramatically reduces bugs, makes collaboration possible, and is exactly what distinguishes a professional data scientist's workflow from an amateur's.
days.practical_task: Take an existing analysis, split it into a tested module, write pytest unit tests for at least three functions including a data-validation test, and configure ruff/black to lint and format the project.
days.mini_project: Convert a project into a properly packaged, tested repository with a src layout, a pytest suite (including a model-quality test), linting configuration, and pinned dependencies managed by uv or poetry.
days.assignment: Write a pytest suite for a data-cleaning function that includes tests for edge cases (empty input, all-null column, unexpected type) and explain your test choices.

quiz.question: Why write tests for your data, not just your code?
options: Because bad or unexpected data silently breaks models and pipelines
quiz.answer: Because bad or unexpected data silently breaks models and pipelines

quiz.question: What does mocking allow you to do in a test?
options: Test code without calling real external services
quiz.answer: Test code without calling real external services

days.expected_outcome: You can build tested, maintainable, reproducible data science codebases.

days.title: High-Performance NumPy and Vectorization
objectives: Write vectorized code that avoids Python loops
days.intermediate_explanation: NumPy is fast because its operations run in optimized C under the hood, but only if you use it correctly, and intermediate practitioners must learn to write vectorized code that replaces slow Python loops with array operations that process entire arrays at once, often 10-100x faster. The key mechanism is broadcasting: NumPy's rules for automatically expanding arrays of different shapes so they can be combined without explicit loops or copies. Understanding memory layout matters too: arrays are stored as contiguous blocks, and 'views' share memory with the original while 'copies' don't, which affects both performance and subtle bugs when you modify data. Strides describe how NumPy steps through memory, and knowing this explains why some operations are cheap and others expensive. Universal functions (ufuncs) apply element-wise operations efficiently, and aggregations along axes summarize data fast. Finally, you learn to profile code to find bottlenecks and optimize the parts that matter. These skills are foundational because Pandas, scikit-learn, and deep learning frameworks all build on NumPy, and vectorized thinking is essential for handling real dataset sizes efficiently.
days.practical_task: Take a computation written with nested Python loops and rewrite it as fully vectorized NumPy using broadcasting, then benchmark both versions to measure the speedup.
days.mini_project: Implement a small numerical routine (e.g., pairwise distance matrix or a moving-window statistic) in three ways - pure Python, partially vectorized, and fully vectorized - and produce a benchmark report explaining the differences.
days.assignment: Demonstrate broadcasting by combining arrays of three different shapes, and write an explanation of how NumPy aligns their dimensions.

quiz.question: Why is vectorized NumPy code faster than an equivalent Python loop?
options: The operations run in optimized compiled C on whole arrays
It uses the GPU by default
quiz.answer: The operations run in optimized compiled C on whole arrays

quiz.question: What is broadcasting?
options: Automatically expanding arrays of different shapes to combine them
quiz.answer: Automatically expanding arrays of different shapes to combine them

days.expected_outcome: You can write fast, vectorized numerical code and reason about NumPy performance.

days.title: Advanced Pandas and Performance
objectives: Optimize memory and speed in Pandas
Handle complex reshaping and multi-index data
days.intermediate_explanation: Beyond basic filtering and grouping, Pandas has a deep toolkit that intermediate data scientists use to handle real, messy, large datasets. MultiIndex (hierarchical indexing) lets you work with data organized along multiple dimensions, like time and region, in a single DataFrame. Advanced groupby goes well past simple aggregation: transform returns group-wise results aligned to the original rows (useful for group-normalization), agg applies multiple functions at once, and apply handles arbitrary group logic. Reshaping with pivot, melt, stack, and unstack lets you move fluidly between wide and long formats, which is essential for analysis and plotting. Performance becomes critical as data grows: choosing efficient dtypes, converting repetitive string columns to categoricals, and downcasting numerics can cut memory use by an order of magnitude and speed up operations. Finally, method chaining and the .pipe pattern let you express a whole transformation as a readable, top-to-bottom sequence without messy intermediate variables. Mastering these turns Pandas from a beginner tool into a powerful engine for serious data manipulation.
days.practical_task: Take a wide dataset, reshape it with melt/pivot, build a MultiIndex, apply a group-wise transform to normalize values within groups, and reduce its memory footprint using categoricals and downcast dtypes.
days.mini_project: Refactor a messy multi-step Pandas analysis into a single readable method-chained pipeline using .pipe, and produce a before/after report on memory usage and execution time after dtype optimization.
days.assignment: Demonstrate the difference between groupby transform and agg on the same dataset, explaining when each is the right tool.

quiz.question: What does groupby().transform() return?
options: Results aligned to the original rows
quiz.answer: Results aligned to the original rows

quiz.question: Which change most reduces the memory of a repetitive string column?
quiz.answer: Converting it to a categorical

days.expected_outcome: You can manipulate complex datasets efficiently and write clean Pandas pipelines.

days.title: Scalable Data Wrangling with Polars and Dask
objectives: Use Polars for fast DataFrame operations
topics: Limits of Pandas and when to scale
days.intermediate_explanation: Pandas is single-threaded and holds everything in memory, so it hits a wall on large datasets, and intermediate data scientists must know how to scale beyond it. Polars is a modern DataFrame library written in Rust that is dramatically faster, uses multiple cores automatically, and offers a lazy execution mode: instead of running each step immediately, it builds a query plan and optimizes the whole thing (like pushing filters earlier) before executing, much like a database. This lazy, query-optimized approach lets Polars process data far larger and faster than Pandas. Dask takes a different approach: it parallelizes familiar Pandas-like operations across cores or even a cluster, and processes data out-of-core, in chunks that don't all fit in memory at once, so you can work with datasets bigger than RAM. You also learn chunked processing patterns for reading and aggregating huge files piece by piece. Knowing when and how to reach for Polars (speed and memory on one machine) versus Dask (parallel/distributed and out-of-core) lets you handle real-world data volumes that would crash a naive Pandas workflow.
days.practical_task: Rewrite a Pandas aggregation pipeline in Polars using lazy execution, then process a larger-than-memory CSV with Dask in chunks, comparing runtime and memory to the Pandas version.
days.mini_project: Take a large dataset and build the same analytical pipeline three ways - Pandas, Polars (lazy), and Dask - then produce a benchmark comparing speed, memory, and code ergonomics, with a recommendation.
days.assignment: Explain, with a concrete example, the difference between Polars eager and lazy execution and why lazy mode can be faster.

quiz.question: What is a key advantage of Polars lazy execution?
options: It builds and optimizes a query plan before running
It removes the need for a schema
It stores data on disk only
quiz.answer: It builds and optimizes a query plan before running

quiz.question: What problem does Dask's out-of-core processing solve?
options: Working with datasets larger than available memory
quiz.answer: Working with datasets larger than available memory

days.title: Advanced SQL and Analytical Queries
days.intermediate_explanation: SQL remains the backbone of analytics, and intermediate data scientists must go far beyond SELECT and GROUP BY to answer sophisticated business questions directly in the database, where it's fastest. Window functions are the centerpiece: they compute values across a set of rows related to the current row (a 'window') without collapsing them, enabling running totals, moving averages, rankings, and period-over-period comparisons using PARTITION BY to define groups and frame clauses to define the window's extent. Recursive CTEs let you query hierarchical data like org charts or category trees. Analytical patterns built on these, cohort analysis (tracking groups over time), funnel analysis (conversion through stages), and retention, are exactly what product and business teams ask for. Equally important is performance: understanding how the database plans and executes a query, reading the execution plan, and using indexes appropriately can turn a query that takes minutes into one that takes milliseconds. Mastering analytical SQL means you can do heavy lifting at the source and hand Python only the results it needs, which is both faster and more scalable.
days.practical_task: Using a database, write queries that compute a running total and a rank within groups using window functions, build a cohort retention query, and inspect a query's execution plan to identify an indexing opportunity.
days.mini_project: Build an analytical SQL report suite for a sample database that includes a funnel analysis, a cohort/retention table, and period-over-period growth, all using window functions and CTEs.
days.assignment: Write a window-function query that computes each row's value as a percentage of its group total, and explain how PARTITION BY drives the result.

quiz.question: What do window functions let you do that GROUP BY does not?
options: Compute across related rows without collapsing them
quiz.answer: Compute across related rows without collapsing them

quiz.question: What is the purpose of reading a query execution plan?
options: To understand and optimize how the database runs the query
quiz.answer: To understand and optimize how the database runs the query

days.title: Data Engineering Fundamentals for Data Scientists
objectives: Work with common data formats and storage
days.intermediate_explanation: Data scientists increasingly need data engineering skills because models are only as good as the data pipelines feeding them. The core concept is ETL/ELT: Extract data from sources, Transform it into a usable shape, and Load it into a store, with modern 'ELT' loading raw data first and transforming inside a powerful warehouse. You learn the difference between batch processing (periodic, large jobs) and streaming (continuous, low-latency ingestion) and when each fits. File formats matter enormously at scale: columnar formats like Parquet are far more efficient than CSV for analytics because they compress well and let you read only the columns you need. You also map the storage landscape, data lakes (raw, any-format storage), warehouses (structured, query-optimized), and lakehouses (combining both), so you know where data lives and why. Crucially, you learn data quality and validation: tools like Great Expectations let you codify expectations (schemas, ranges, uniqueness) so bad data is caught before it corrupts analyses or models. Understanding these fundamentals lets a data scientist build reliable pipelines, collaborate effectively with data engineers, and take ownership of the full data lifecycle.
days.practical_task: Build a small ETL pipeline in Python that ingests raw data, transforms and validates it (with basic quality checks), and writes it out as partitioned Parquet, comparing file size and read speed to CSV.
days.mini_project: Create a reproducible ingestion pipeline that pulls data from a source, applies Great Expectations validation rules, transforms it, and loads it into a local warehouse (e.g., DuckDB), failing loudly on data-quality violations.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between ETL and ELT with a concrete example, and describe when you would choose Parquet over CSV.

quiz.question: Why is Parquet often preferred over CSV for analytics at scale?
options: It is columnar, compresses well, and allows reading only needed columns
quiz.answer: It is columnar, compresses well, and allows reading only needed columns

quiz.question: What does a tool like Great Expectations provide?
options: Codified data-quality validation to catch bad data early
quiz.answer: Codified data-quality validation to catch bad data early

days.title: Data Modeling and Analytics Engineering with dbt
objectives: Apply testing and documentation to transformations
topics: The modern data stack and dbt
days.intermediate_explanation: Raw data is rarely analysis-ready, and analytics engineering is the discipline of transforming it into clean, well-structured models that analysts and data scientists can trust. The foundational technique is dimensional modeling: organizing data into fact tables (measurable events like sales, each row a transaction) and dimension tables (descriptive context like customers, products, dates), arranged in a star schema that is intuitive and fast to query. dbt (data build tool) has become the standard for this in the 'modern data stack': you write transformations as SQL SELECT statements (models), reference other models with ref() so dbt builds a dependency graph and runs them in the right order, and choose materializations (view, table, incremental) to balance freshness and cost. Crucially, dbt brings software engineering to analytics, you write tests (uniqueness, not-null, referential integrity) that run on every build to catch data problems, and you auto-generate documentation and lineage graphs. Learning dbt and dimensional modeling lets a data scientist produce reliable, documented, tested datasets, bridging the gap between raw pipelines and the clean tables that power analysis and features.
days.practical_task: Design a small star schema for a sample business, then implement it in dbt with staging and mart models using ref(), add not-null and unique tests, and generate the documentation site.
days.mini_project: Build a dbt project that transforms raw source tables into a documented, tested star schema (facts and dimensions), including at least one incremental model and a lineage graph, on a local warehouse like DuckDB.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between a fact table and a dimension table with an example, and describe why star schemas are efficient to query.

quiz.question: In dimensional modeling, what does a fact table typically contain?
quiz.answer: Measurable business events/metrics

quiz.question: What does dbt's ref() function enable?
options: Building a dependency graph so models run in the correct order
quiz.answer: Building a dependency graph so models run in the correct order

days.title: Advanced Probability and Distributions
objectives: Work with joint, marginal, and conditional distributions
Apply key distributions to real problems
days.intermediate_explanation: Intermediate data science demands a deeper, working command of probability than the beginner rules. You solidify random variables and their expectation and variance, then move to multivariate thinking: joint distributions describe two or more variables together, marginal distributions isolate one by summing out the others, and conditional distributions describe one given the value of another, the machinery behind everything from Naive Bayes to graphical models. You develop fluency with the distributions you'll actually meet, when data is Poisson (counts), exponential (waiting times), gamma, beta (proportions), or multivariate normal, and why. The conceptual centerpiece is Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE): given data and an assumed distribution, MLE finds the parameters that make the observed data most probable by maximizing the likelihood function. This is the theoretical foundation under most models you fit, linear regression, logistic regression, and many more are MLE in disguise, so understanding it demystifies why models are trained the way they are. This deeper probability foundation makes advanced statistics, Bayesian methods, and probabilistic modeling accessible rather than mysterious.
days.practical_task: For a chosen dataset, fit a distribution's parameters using MLE (analytically or with scipy.optimize), overlay the fitted density on a histogram, and compute conditional distributions from a joint frequency table.
days.mini_project: Build a notebook that, given a real dataset, identifies plausible distributions for several variables, fits them via MLE, and uses goodness-of-fit checks to compare candidates, with written interpretation.
days.assignment: Derive or explain why ordinary least squares regression is equivalent to MLE under Gaussian noise, in your own words.

quiz.answer: Parameters that make the observed data most probable

quiz.question: A marginal distribution is obtained from a joint distribution by:
quiz.answer: Summing/integrating out the other variables

days.title: Statistical Inference and Resampling
objectives: Perform robust inference beyond textbook tests
days.intermediate_explanation: Real data rarely satisfies the neat assumptions of textbook tests, so intermediate practitioners rely on resampling methods that make far fewer assumptions. The bootstrap is the key idea: by repeatedly resampling your data with replacement and recomputing a statistic, you empirically build its sampling distribution and derive confidence intervals for almost any statistic, even ones with no simple formula. Permutation tests take a similar computational approach to hypothesis testing: by randomly shuffling labels many times, you build the distribution of a test statistic under the null hypothesis and compute a p-value directly, without distributional assumptions. Just as important is handling multiple comparisons: when you run many tests, some will look significant by chance, so you correct for it, Bonferroni controls the family-wise error rate conservatively, while the False Discovery Rate (Benjamini-Hochberg) is a more powerful modern choice. Finally, you learn to report effect size (how big the difference is, not just whether it's significant) and to reason about statistical power (the chance of detecting a real effect). These tools make your inferences trustworthy on messy, real-world data.
days.practical_task: Compute a bootstrap confidence interval for a statistic that lacks a simple closed form (e.g., the median or a correlation), and run a permutation test to compare two groups, comparing results to a classical test.
days.mini_project: Build a resampling-based inference toolkit notebook that provides bootstrap CIs and permutation tests, applies FDR correction across many comparisons on a real dataset, and reports effect sizes.
days.assignment: Explain why running 20 independent tests at alpha=0.05 tends to produce a false positive, and show how a correction fixes it.

options: By resampling the data with replacement many times
quiz.answer: By resampling the data with replacement many times

quiz.question: Why apply a multiple-testing correction?
options: Because running many tests inflates the chance of false positives
quiz.answer: Because running many tests inflates the chance of false positives

days.title: Bayesian Statistics and Inference
days.intermediate_explanation: Bayesian statistics offers a powerful alternative to the frequentist methods you've used, treating probability as a degree of belief that you update with evidence. The framework is Bayes' theorem applied to parameters: you start with a prior distribution encoding what you believe before seeing data, combine it with the likelihood of the observed data, and get a posterior distribution representing your updated belief. This gives you full distributions over parameters, not just point estimates, so uncertainty is built in. Conjugate priors make some updates analytic, but most real models require sampling from the posterior, which is where Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) comes in: it draws samples that approximate the posterior even when it has no closed form. Probabilistic programming languages like PyMC let you specify a model declaratively and handle the sampling automatically, making Bayesian modeling practical. A key conceptual payoff is interpretation: a Bayesian 95% credible interval genuinely means 'there's a 95% probability the parameter lies in this range,' which is what people wrongly assume confidence intervals mean. Bayesian methods shine with small data, prior knowledge, and when honest uncertainty quantification matters.
days.practical_task: Model a simple problem (e.g., estimating a conversion rate) in PyMC with a prior and likelihood, sample the posterior, and report a credible interval, comparing it to the frequentist estimate.
days.mini_project: Build a Bayesian analysis of an A/B test in PyMC that models both groups, computes the posterior probability that B beats A, and visualizes the posteriors with credible intervals.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between a 95% credible interval and a 95% confidence interval, and why the Bayesian interpretation is often what people actually want.

quiz.question: In Bayesian inference, the posterior is proportional to:
quiz.answer: Prior times likelihood

quiz.question: What does MCMC provide?
options: Samples that approximate the posterior distribution
quiz.answer: Samples that approximate the posterior distribution

days.title: Advanced Experiment Design and A/B Testing
topics: Power analysis and sample size determination
days.intermediate_explanation: Beyond a basic two-group comparison, real experimentation at companies requires rigor and cleverness, and this is a highly valued intermediate skill. It starts with power analysis: before running a test you compute the sample size needed to reliably detect a minimum effect worth caring about, so you don't waste time on underpowered tests. Good design controls nuisance variation through randomization, stratification (ensuring groups are balanced on key covariates), and blocking. A major practical technique is variance reduction: methods like CUPED use pre-experiment data to remove noise, letting you reach significance with far fewer users. You confront the 'peeking' problem, checking results repeatedly inflates false positives, and its solutions: sequential testing methods that allow valid continuous monitoring. Finally, you learn when a fixed A/B test is the right tool versus a multi-armed bandit, which dynamically shifts traffic toward better-performing variants to reduce the cost of experimentation. Mastering these techniques lets you design experiments that are fast, trustworthy, and efficient, exactly what product and growth teams depend on data scientists to deliver.
days.practical_task: Run a power analysis to determine the sample size for a target effect, then simulate an experiment and apply CUPED variance reduction, comparing the required sample size with and without it.
days.mini_project: Build an experimentation analysis notebook that designs a test (power/sample size), analyzes results with variance reduction, and includes a simulation demonstrating why naive peeking inflates false positives.
days.assignment: Explain when you would prefer a multi-armed bandit over a classic A/B test, giving a concrete business scenario.

quiz.answer: The sample size needed to detect a meaningful effect

quiz.answer: They reach significance with fewer samples

days.title: Causal Inference
days.intermediate_explanation: Businesses constantly ask causal questions, 'did this campaign cause more sales?', but you can't always run an experiment, so causal inference gives you tools to estimate cause and effect from observational data. The conceptual foundation is potential outcomes and counterfactuals: the causal effect of a treatment is the difference between what happened and what would have happened without it, which we never directly observe. The central obstacle is confounding, a third variable that influences both treatment and outcome and creates spurious association; causal graphs (DAGs) help you reason visually about which variables to control for and which to leave alone. Several methods let you estimate effects despite confounding: propensity score matching pairs treated and untreated units with similar characteristics; difference-in-differences compares changes over time between a treated and control group, cancelling out fixed differences; instrumental variables exploit a factor that affects treatment but not the outcome directly; and regression discontinuity uses a cutoff rule to approximate an experiment. These methods, used carefully, let a data scientist move from 'these are correlated' to defensible claims about what actually drives outcomes, which is enormously valuable and increasingly expected.
days.practical_task: On an observational dataset, estimate a treatment effect two ways - a naive comparison and propensity score matching - and explain why the estimates differ due to confounding.
days.mini_project: Build a causal analysis that draws a DAG for a real question, chooses an appropriate method (matching or difference-in-differences), estimates the causal effect, and discusses assumptions and threats to validity.
days.assignment: Take a real headline claiming causation from observational data and critique it using the concepts of confounding and counterfactuals.

days.title: Advanced Feature Engineering
days.intermediate_explanation: Feature engineering is often the biggest lever on model performance, and intermediate practitioners apply it systematically and safely. Beyond one-hot encoding, you handle high-cardinality categoricals with target encoding (replacing a category with the mean target for that category) and frequency encoding, which capture signal compactly, but you must do this carefully within cross-validation folds to avoid leakage. You create feature interactions and polynomial terms to let linear models capture non-linear relationships. For time-aware problems you engineer temporal features (day of week, seasonality) and lag/rolling features that summarize the recent past, which are critical for forecasting and any time-dependent target. You build aggregation features that summarize related records (a customer's average order value, count of past events) and extract structured signal from text. The unifying discipline is preventing data leakage: any feature computed using information that wouldn't be available at prediction time, or fit on the whole dataset before splitting, will inflate offline metrics and fail in production. So you build leakage-safe pipelines where every feature transformation is fit only on training data within each fold. Mastering this turns raw data into the informative features that separate winning models from mediocre ones.
days.practical_task: On a dataset with high-cardinality categoricals and a timestamp, engineer target-encoded, lag, and aggregation features inside a cross-validation-safe pipeline, and verify no leakage inflates the score.
days.mini_project: Build an advanced feature engineering pipeline for a predictive problem that combines encoding, interactions, temporal/lag features, and aggregations, then quantify the model-performance lift each feature group provides.
days.assignment: Show, with an experiment, how naive target encoding on the full dataset leaks and inflates validation performance, and fix it with proper in-fold encoding.

quiz.question: Why must target encoding be done inside cross-validation folds?
options: To avoid leaking target information into the features
To reduce the number of categories
quiz.answer: To avoid leaking target information into the features

quiz.question: What are lag features useful for?
options: Capturing information from previous time steps
quiz.answer: Capturing information from previous time steps

days.title: Feature Selection and Dimensionality Reduction
objectives: Reduce dimensionality with linear and nonlinear methods
topics: Filter, wrapper, and embedded selection methods
days.intermediate_explanation: More features are not always better, too many can cause overfitting, slow training, and obscure interpretation, so intermediate practitioners actively select and reduce them. Feature selection comes in three families: filter methods rank features by a statistical measure (correlation, mutual information) independent of any model; wrapper methods like recursive feature elimination repeatedly train a model and drop the weakest features; and embedded methods select during training, most notably L1 (Lasso) regularization, which drives some coefficients exactly to zero, performing selection automatically. Dimensionality reduction takes a different route, compressing many features into fewer combined components. You revisit PCA (linear) and meet kernel PCA for nonlinear structure. For visualization and understanding high-dimensional data, nonlinear methods like t-SNE and UMAP project data into 2D while preserving local structure, revealing clusters that linear methods miss (though they're for exploration, not as model inputs). The art is balancing predictive performance, computational cost, and interpretability: fewer, well-chosen features often generalize better and are far easier to explain to stakeholders.
days.practical_task: On a high-dimensional dataset, compare feature subsets chosen by a filter method, recursive feature elimination, and L1 regularization, and use UMAP to visualize the data in 2D.
days.mini_project: Build a feature-selection study that reduces a wide dataset with three different methods, measures the effect on model performance and training time, and produces a UMAP visualization of the resulting structure.
days.assignment: Explain how L1 regularization performs feature selection and contrast it with recursive feature elimination.

quiz.answer: L1 (Lasso)

quiz.answer: Visualizing high-dimensional data by preserving local structure

days.title: Regularized and Generalized Linear Models
days.intermediate_explanation: Linear models remain indispensable because they're fast, interpretable, and, with the right techniques, surprisingly competitive. Regularization adds a penalty on coefficient size to the loss, preventing the model from fitting noise and thus controlling overfitting, directly managing the bias-variance tradeoff. Ridge (L2) shrinks all coefficients smoothly and handles correlated features well; Lasso (L1) shrinks some to exactly zero, performing feature selection; and Elastic Net blends both, giving you the benefits of each. You tune the regularization strength with cross-validation and can visualize the regularization path to see how coefficients shrink as the penalty grows. Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) extend linear regression to non-normal targets by using a link function: logistic regression (for binary outcomes) is a GLM, Poisson regression models count data, and others handle rates and proportions. Choosing the right GLM for your target's distribution produces well-calibrated, interpretable models, for example, Poisson regression for predicting event counts. Together, regularization and GLMs give you a principled, transparent modeling toolkit that's often the right first choice and a strong, explainable baseline for any problem.
days.practical_task: Fit Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net on a dataset with correlated features, tune the penalty via cross-validation, plot the regularization path, and fit a Poisson GLM to a count target.
days.mini_project: Build a comparison of regularized linear models on a real dataset that tunes each, reports which features Lasso keeps, and includes a GLM appropriate to the target's distribution with interpretation.
days.assignment: Explain how increasing the regularization strength affects bias and variance, illustrated with your cross-validation curves.

quiz.answer: Lasso (L1)

quiz.answer: Modeling non-normal targets like counts or probabilities

days.title: Classification with Imbalanced Data
topics: Why accuracy fails on imbalanced data
days.intermediate_explanation: Many high-value problems, fraud, churn, disease, rare-event detection, have severely imbalanced classes, and standard approaches fail badly here. A model that predicts 'not fraud' for everyone can be 99.9% accurate and completely useless, which is why accuracy is the wrong metric; you use precision, recall, F1, and especially the precision-recall curve and average precision, which focus on the rare positive class. Several techniques help models learn from few positives: resampling methods like SMOTE synthesize new minority examples or undersample the majority to balance training; class weights and cost-sensitive learning tell the algorithm to penalize errors on the rare class more heavily, which is often cleaner than resampling. Crucially, you decouple the model from the decision threshold: instead of the default 0.5, you tune the threshold to the business tradeoff between catching positives (recall) and avoiding false alarms (precision). Finally, probability calibration ensures the scores a model outputs actually reflect real probabilities, essential when downstream decisions depend on '70% likely' meaning what it says. Handling imbalance well is a hallmark of a capable applied data scientist because so many real problems have this structure.
days.practical_task: On an imbalanced dataset, compare a baseline, class-weighting, and SMOTE, evaluate with precision-recall and average precision, tune the decision threshold to a business objective, and check calibration.
days.mini_project: Build a fraud/churn-style classifier on imbalanced data that applies at least two imbalance techniques, selects an operating threshold from the precision-recall tradeoff, and produces a calibration plot with interpretation.
days.assignment: Explain with an example why a 99% accurate model can be worthless on imbalanced data and which metrics you would report instead.

days.title: Ensemble Methods Deep Dive
objectives: Understand bagging, boosting, and stacking deeply
Know when ensembles help or hurt
days.intermediate_explanation: Ensembles combine multiple models to outperform any single one, and understanding them deeply, not just calling RandomForest, lets you squeeze out top performance. The unifying lens is the bias-variance tradeoff. Bagging (bootstrap aggregating), the basis of Random Forests, trains many high-variance models on resampled data and averages them, primarily reducing variance. Boosting works oppositely: it trains weak models sequentially, each correcting the previous ones' errors, primarily reducing bias to build a strong learner (the foundation of gradient boosting you'll master next). Stacking is the most sophisticated: you train several diverse base models and then a 'meta-model' that learns how to best combine their predictions, often beating any base model. Simpler blending and voting average predictions directly. The key insight running through all of this is diversity: ensembles help most when their members make different, uncorrelated errors, so combining a tree model, a linear model, and a neural network can beat combining three similar trees. You also learn when ensembles aren't worth it, when interpretability, latency, or simplicity matters more than a marginal accuracy gain. This deeper understanding lets you engineer ensembles deliberately rather than by trial and error.
days.practical_task: Build a stacking ensemble that combines at least three diverse base models with a meta-learner, and compare its cross-validated performance to each base model and to simple voting.
days.mini_project: Build an ensemble study on a dataset that implements bagging, boosting, voting, and stacking, analyzes the correlation of base-model errors, and explains why diversity did or didn't help.
days.assignment: Explain, using the bias-variance framework, why bagging reduces variance while boosting reduces bias.

quiz.answer: Variance

quiz.answer: Uncorrelated errors cancel out when combined

days.title: Gradient Boosting Mastery
objectives: Handle categoricals and large data efficiently
topics: How gradient boosting works under the hood
Handling categoricals and missing values natively
days.intermediate_explanation: Gradient boosting is the reigning champion for tabular data, and mastering it is one of the highest-return skills for an applied data scientist. Under the hood, it builds trees sequentially, each fitting the negative gradient (the direction that reduces the loss) of the previous ensemble's errors, effectively doing gradient descent in function space. The three dominant libraries differ in ways that matter: XGBoost is robust and widely used; LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise and uses histogram binning for blazing speed on large data; CatBoost handles categorical features natively with clever ordered encoding that avoids leakage. You learn the hyperparameters that actually move the needle, learning rate, number of estimators, tree depth/leaves, subsampling, and the regularization terms (L1/L2 and minimum child weight) that combat overfitting, and how they interact (lower learning rate needs more trees). These models also handle missing values and, in CatBoost/LightGBM, categoricals natively, reducing preprocessing. You use early stopping against a validation set to find the right number of trees automatically and read learning curves to diagnose over/underfitting. With expert tuning, gradient boosting delivers state-of-the-art tabular performance, which is why it dominates competitions and production systems alike.
days.practical_task: Train XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost on the same tabular problem, tune the core hyperparameters with early stopping, and compare accuracy, speed, and preprocessing effort across the three.

days.assignment: Explain how the learning rate and number of estimators trade off, supported by learning curves at two different learning rates.

quiz.answer: The errors/negative gradient of the current ensemble

quiz.answer: It uses histogram binning and leaf-wise tree growth

days.title: Hyperparameter Optimization at Scale
topics: Grid vs random vs Bayesian search
days.intermediate_explanation: As models gain many hyperparameters, brute-force grid search becomes hopelessly inefficient, so intermediate practitioners use smarter optimization. Random search is a surprisingly strong baseline because it explores important dimensions better than a grid. Bayesian optimization is the modern standard: it builds a probabilistic model of how hyperparameters affect performance and uses it to intelligently choose the next configuration to try, focusing effort on promising regions, Optuna implements this with Tree-structured Parzen Estimators and a clean define-by-run API. Multi-fidelity methods like Hyperband and Optuna's pruning save enormous compute by stopping unpromising trials early (e.g., killing a run after a few epochs if it's clearly losing), so you evaluate many more configurations for the same budget. You also learn to define good search spaces, using log scales for learning rates, conditional parameters that only apply for certain choices, and how to parallelize or distribute the search across cores or machines. Finally, you track every trial so results are reproducible and comparable. Efficient hyperparameter optimization can be the difference between a good model and a great one, delivered in a fraction of the time.
days.practical_task: Use Optuna to tune a gradient boosting model with a well-designed search space, enable pruning to stop weak trials early, and compare the best result and time to a grid search.
days.mini_project: Build a reusable Optuna tuning framework for a model that defines a conditional search space, uses pruning, runs trials in parallel, logs all trials, and visualizes the optimization history and parameter importances.
days.assignment: Explain why Bayesian optimization is more sample-efficient than grid search, and describe what pruning saves.

quiz.answer: Stops unpromising trials early to save compute

days.title: Rigorous Model Evaluation and Validation
objectives: Choose validation strategies for the data structure
days.intermediate_explanation: Trustworthy model evaluation is deceptively hard, and getting it wrong is one of the most common ways real projects fail silently. The core danger is optimistic bias: if you tune hyperparameters and estimate performance on the same data, your reported score is inflated. Nested cross-validation fixes this by using an inner loop for tuning and an outer loop for unbiased performance estimation. Equally important is matching the validation scheme to the data's structure: for time series you must use time-based splits (train on the past, test on the future) because random splits leak future information; for data with groups (multiple rows per customer) you use grouped splits so the same entity never appears in both train and test. You develop a sharp eye for leakage, features or preprocessing that sneak target or future information into training, which is the number-one cause of models that look brilliant offline and fail in production. Beyond a single number, you quantify uncertainty by putting confidence intervals on metrics and statistically comparing models to know whether a difference is real or noise. Rigorous validation is what lets you deploy with confidence rather than hope.
days.practical_task: Implement nested cross-validation for a tuned model, then build a correct time-series (forward-chaining) split for a temporal dataset, contrasting the honest score with a naive random-split score.
days.mini_project: Build a validation harness that supports nested CV, grouped CV, and time-series CV, reports metrics with bootstrap confidence intervals, and statistically compares two candidate models.
days.assignment: Give a concrete example of data leakage in a time-series problem and explain how a proper split prevents it.

options: To get an unbiased performance estimate when also tuning hyperparameters
quiz.answer: To get an unbiased performance estimate when also tuning hyperparameters

quiz.question: Why must time-series data use time-based splits?
options: Because random splits leak future information into training
quiz.answer: Because random splits leak future information into training

days.title: Model Interpretability and Explainability
objectives: Apply SHAP and other explainability tools
days.intermediate_explanation: Powerful models like gradient boosting and neural networks are often 'black boxes,' but stakeholders, regulators, and your own debugging demand explanations, so interpretability is now an essential skill. You distinguish two levels: global explanations describe how the model behaves overall (which features matter most, how the prediction changes with a feature), while local explanations justify a single prediction ('this loan was denied mainly because of X and Y'). Permutation importance measures how much shuffling a feature hurts performance, and partial dependence plots show a feature's average effect. The centerpiece is SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), grounded in cooperative game theory: it fairly attributes each prediction to its features with strong consistency guarantees, and provides both local explanations and, aggregated, global insight, with rich visualizations. LIME offers fast local approximations, and counterfactual explanations tell you the smallest change that would flip a decision, which is intuitive and actionable. Beyond communication, interpretability is a debugging tool, it exposes leakage, spurious correlations, and bias. Mastering these techniques lets you deploy complex models responsibly and build trust with the people who depend on them.
days.practical_task: Train a gradient boosting model and explain it with permutation importance, partial dependence plots, and SHAP (both a global summary plot and a local explanation for individual predictions).

days.assignment: Use SHAP to investigate a suspiciously high-performing model and determine whether an unexpectedly important feature indicates leakage.

days.title: Advanced Clustering and Unsupervised Learning
days.intermediate_explanation: K-Means is a fine starting point but has real limitations: it assumes spherical, similarly-sized clusters, requires you to specify the number of clusters, and forces every point into a cluster, so intermediate practitioners learn a broader toolkit. Density-based methods like DBSCAN and HDBSCAN find clusters of arbitrary shape by grouping densely-packed points and, crucially, label sparse points as noise/outliers, and they don't need you to pre-specify the number of clusters, making them excellent for messy real data. Gaussian Mixture Models take a probabilistic, model-based view: they assume the data is generated from a mixture of Gaussian distributions and perform soft clustering, giving each point a probability of belonging to each cluster, which is more nuanced than hard assignment. Hierarchical clustering builds a tree (dendrogram) of nested clusters, letting you choose the granularity after the fact and understand structure at multiple scales. Because clustering is unsupervised, validating it is subtle: internal metrics like the silhouette score and Davies-Bouldin index assess cohesion and separation without labels. Knowing which algorithm fits the data's geometry, and how to validate the result, lets you extract genuine structure rather than imposing artificial groupings.
days.practical_task: On a dataset with non-spherical clusters and noise, compare K-Means, DBSCAN/HDBSCAN, and a Gaussian Mixture Model, and use silhouette and Davies-Bouldin scores plus visualization to judge them.

days.title: Anomaly and Outlier Detection
topics: Local Outlier Factor and one-class SVM
days.intermediate_explanation: Anomaly detection, finding the rare, unusual, or suspicious, powers fraud detection, fault monitoring, network security, and data-quality checks, and it's challenging because anomalies are rare and often unlabeled. You first distinguish types: point anomalies (a single unusual value), contextual anomalies (normal in general but abnormal in context, like high heating usage in summer), and collective anomalies (a sequence that's abnormal together). Simple statistical methods, z-scores, the IQR rule, and the Mahalanobis distance for multivariate data, catch many cases cheaply. Machine-learning detectors handle complex, high-dimensional data: Isolation Forest isolates anomalies efficiently because they're easier to separate with random splits; Local Outlier Factor flags points in low-density regions relative to their neighbors; and one-class SVM learns a boundary around 'normal' data. For rich data, autoencoders (neural networks that learn to reconstruct normal data) flag anomalies as points they reconstruct poorly. A recurring difficulty is evaluation: with few or no labels, you rely on precision-recall on whatever labels exist, expert review, and the business cost of false positives versus misses. Mastering anomaly detection lets you build the monitoring and safety systems that many high-value applications depend on.
days.practical_task: Apply a statistical method, Isolation Forest, and Local Outlier Factor to a dataset with known anomalies, compare which anomalies each catches, and evaluate with precision-recall on the labeled subset.
days.mini_project: Build an anomaly detection system for a realistic scenario (fraud, sensor faults, or data-quality) that combines at least two methods, tunes the contamination/threshold to a cost tradeoff, and reports detection performance.
days.assignment: Give a concrete example of a contextual anomaly and explain why a simple global threshold would miss it.

days.title: Recommender Systems
days.intermediate_explanation: Recommender systems drive a huge share of value at consumer companies, deciding what products, videos, or content users see, so it's a high-impact specialization. Two classic approaches anchor the field. Content-based filtering recommends items similar to those a user liked, using item features, it handles new items well but can be narrow. Collaborative filtering recommends based on patterns across users ('people like you also liked...') without needing item features, and it's remarkably powerful; its workhorse is matrix factorization (SVD, ALS), which learns latent factors for users and items whose dot products predict preferences. You confront real challenges: most feedback is implicit (clicks, watches) rather than explicit ratings, requiring different modeling; and the cold-start problem, how to recommend for brand-new users or items with no history, often solved with hybrid methods that blend content and collaborative signals. Evaluation differs from standard ML: because recommendations are ranked lists, you use ranking metrics like Precision@k, NDCG, and MAP that reward putting relevant items near the top. Understanding these fundamentals prepares you for both classic systems and modern deep-learning recommenders, and for the offline/online evaluation gap that makes recommendation uniquely tricky.
days.practical_task: Build a collaborative-filtering recommender with matrix factorization on a ratings dataset (e.g., MovieLens) and a content-based recommender, then evaluate both with Precision@k and NDCG.

days.assignment: Explain the cold-start problem and describe two concrete strategies to mitigate it.

days.title: Time Series Forecasting: Classical Methods
objectives: Model and forecast time series rigorously
days.intermediate_explanation: Forecasting, demand, revenue, traffic, is one of data science's most requested capabilities, and the classical ARIMA family remains a rigorous, interpretable foundation. The key prerequisite is stationarity: many models assume the series' statistical properties don't change over time, so you test for it (e.g., the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test) and apply differencing to remove trends and make it stationary. To identify model structure you read the autocorrelation (ACF) and partial autocorrelation (PACF) plots, which reveal how many autoregressive (AR) and moving-average (MA) terms to include. ARIMA combines AR, differencing (I), and MA components; SARIMA adds seasonal terms for repeating patterns; and ARIMAX/SARIMAX incorporate exogenous predictors like promotions or holidays. Crucially, you validate forecasts correctly with backtesting, evaluating on rolling out-of-sample windows that respect time order, never a random split, and use forecast-appropriate metrics like MAE, RMSE, and MAPE. You also generate prediction intervals to communicate uncertainty. Mastering classical forecasting gives you transparent, well-understood models that are often competitive with fancier methods and serve as essential baselines for any forecasting project.
days.practical_task: Take a time series, test and enforce stationarity, use ACF/PACF to select an ARIMA/SARIMA order, fit the model, and backtest it with rolling-origin evaluation reporting MAE and MAPE.

days.assignment: Explain why you must never use a random train/test split for forecasting and how rolling-origin backtesting works instead.

days.title: Modern and ML-Based Time Series Forecasting
objectives: Forecast with ML and specialized libraries

topics: Intro to deep learning forecasting (LSTM, N-BEATS)
days.intermediate_explanation: Classical models shine on single, well-behaved series, but modern practice often needs to forecast many series with rich covariates, and machine learning approaches excel here. The key reframing is turning forecasting into a supervised learning problem: you engineer lag features (past values), rolling statistics, and calendar/seasonal features, then train any regressor, often gradient boosting, to predict the next value. This lets you incorporate arbitrary external drivers (price, weather, promotions) and handle nonlinearities that ARIMA can't. Facebook's Prophet is a popular, robust choice for business series with strong seasonality and holiday effects, requiring little tuning and producing interpretable trend/seasonality decompositions with uncertainty intervals. A major advantage of ML approaches is global models: instead of fitting one model per series, you train a single model across thousands of related series (e.g., sales per store-item), which shares information and scales far better, this is how large retailers forecast. You also get an introduction to deep learning forecasters like LSTMs and N-BEATS that capture complex temporal patterns. Knowing when to use classical, feature-based ML, Prophet, or global/deep models, and how to validate each with proper backtesting, makes you a versatile, scalable forecaster.

days.title: Deep Learning Foundations with PyTorch
objectives: Build and train neural networks in PyTorch
Understand tensors, autograd, and training loops
topics: The training loop, loss, and optimizers
days.intermediate_explanation: PyTorch is the dominant framework for deep learning research and increasingly production, and understanding it from first principles, rather than only high-level Keras, gives you the flexibility serious work demands. The core object is the tensor, a multidimensional array like NumPy's but able to run on GPUs for massive speedups and to track operations for gradients. That gradient tracking is autograd: PyTorch builds a computational graph of your operations and automatically computes derivatives via backpropagation, so you never hand-derive gradients. You define models by subclassing nn.Module, composing layers and specifying the forward pass, which makes architectures explicit and customizable. Then you write the training loop yourself, and doing so demystifies training: for each batch you compute predictions, calculate the loss, call backward() to get gradients, and step the optimizer (like Adam) to update weights, remembering to zero gradients each iteration. Datasets and DataLoaders handle efficient batching, shuffling, and parallel loading. This lower-level control is essential because real deep learning, custom architectures, losses, and training logic, requires understanding each piece, and this foundation underlies everything from CNNs to transformers.

days.title: Neural Network Training and Optimization
objectives: Train deep networks that converge well
topics: Optimizers (SGD, Adam, AdamW) and learning rate schedules
days.intermediate_explanation: Getting a neural network to actually learn well is a craft, and this day covers the techniques that separate a network that trains smoothly from one that stalls or overfits. Optimizers matter: plain SGD works but Adam/AdamW adapt the learning rate per parameter and converge faster, and pairing them with learning-rate schedules (warmup, decay, cosine) improves both speed and final quality. The learning rate is the single most important hyperparameter, too high diverges, too low crawls. Weight initialization affects whether gradients flow: poor initialization causes vanishing or exploding gradients in deep nets, which is why schemes like He/Xavier initialization exist. Batch normalization stabilizes and accelerates training by normalizing activations, and dropout regularizes by randomly disabling neurons so the network can't over-rely on any one path. You combine these with classic regularization (weight decay) and early stopping to prevent overfitting. Just as important is diagnosis: reading loss curves to tell underfitting from overfitting, spotting a bad learning rate, and recognizing when the model isn't learning at all. Mastering these training dynamics lets you reliably get good results instead of fighting mysterious failures, a skill that applies to every deep architecture you'll build.
days.practical_task: Train the same network with different optimizers and learning rates, add batch normalization and dropout, and use loss curves to diagnose and fix an overfitting and an underfitting scenario.
days.mini_project: Build a training-diagnostics notebook that systematically studies the effect of learning rate, optimizer, batch norm, and dropout on a network, with learning-curve visualizations and a written 'training playbook' of best practices.
days.assignment: Describe the symptoms of too-high and too-low learning rates on the loss curve and how you would correct each.

days.title: Convolutional Neural Networks for Computer Vision
days.intermediate_explanation: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) revolutionized computer vision, and understanding them equips you for any image task. The core operation is convolution: small learnable filters slide across the image detecting local patterns, edges in early layers, textures and shapes deeper, producing feature maps; this exploits the spatial structure of images and shares parameters, making CNNs far more efficient than dense networks for images. Pooling layers downsample to build translation-invariance and shrink computation. You study landmark architectures, ResNet's skip connections that enabled very deep networks, and efficient modern designs, to understand the design principles rather than memorize them. Because vision datasets are often limited, two techniques are essential: data augmentation (random flips, crops, color shifts) artificially expands training data and improves generalization; and transfer learning, taking a network pretrained on millions of images (ImageNet) and fine-tuning it on your smaller task, which delivers strong results with far less data and compute than training from scratch. This is how most real vision projects are done. You put it together in a full PyTorch pipeline: data loading with augmentation, a pretrained backbone, fine-tuning, and evaluation, giving you a practical, transferable workflow for image problems.
days.practical_task: Fine-tune a pretrained CNN (e.g., ResNet) on an image classification dataset using data augmentation, and compare its accuracy to a small CNN trained from scratch.
days.mini_project: Build an image classifier for a real task via transfer learning that includes an augmentation pipeline, fine-tuning of a pretrained backbone, evaluation with a confusion matrix, and visualization of misclassified examples.
days.assignment: Explain why transfer learning works so well for images with limited data, and what you would freeze versus fine-tune.

quiz.answer: Local spatial patterns like edges and textures

days.title: Sequence Models: RNNs and LSTMs
objectives: Apply sequence models to real tasks
days.intermediate_explanation: Before transformers, recurrent neural networks (RNNs) were the standard for sequential data, text, time series, audio, and they remain valuable to understand both practically and conceptually. An RNN processes a sequence one step at a time, maintaining a hidden state that carries information forward, so it can, in principle, use past context to interpret the present. But vanilla RNNs suffer from vanishing gradients over long sequences, meaning they forget distant context. LSTMs (Long Short-Term Memory) solve this with a gating mechanism: learnable gates control what to remember, forget, and output, letting information persist across many time steps; GRUs are a lighter variant with similar power. You apply these to real sequence tasks: sequence classification (sentiment), sequence forecasting (predicting future values), and sequence-to-sequence models (translation, where an encoder reads the input and a decoder generates the output). Understanding RNNs/LSTMs also clarifies why transformers took over, their sequential processing prevents parallelization and still struggles with very long-range dependencies, motivating the attention mechanism you'll study next. This grounding makes the leap to modern architectures intuitive rather than magical.
days.practical_task: Build an LSTM in PyTorch for a sequence task (e.g., text sentiment or univariate forecasting), train it, and compare its performance to a non-sequential baseline.
days.mini_project: Build a sequence-modeling project (sentiment classification or forecasting) using an LSTM/GRU, experiment with sequence length and hidden size, and write an analysis of where the model succeeds and fails.
days.assignment: Explain how LSTM gates mitigate the vanishing gradient problem that limits vanilla RNNs.

quiz.answer: Vanishing gradients / forgetting long-range context

quiz.question: Why do RNNs struggle to parallelize?

quiz.answer: They process a sequence step by step, each depending on the previous state

days.title: NLP: Text Processing and Embeddings
objectives: Understand and use word and sentence embeddings
topics: Text classification and clustering with embeddings
days.intermediate_explanation: Modern NLP represents text as dense vectors (embeddings) that capture meaning, and understanding embeddings is foundational to everything from search to LLMs. It begins with tokenization: splitting text into units. Beyond simple word tokens, subword methods like Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) break words into common fragments, which handles rare/unknown words gracefully and is what modern models use. Static word embeddings like Word2Vec and GloVe map each word to a fixed vector learned so that words used in similar contexts sit close together, famously enabling analogies (king - man + woman ~ queen). Their limitation is that a word has one vector regardless of context, which contextual embeddings (from models like BERT) fix by producing vectors that depend on the surrounding sentence, so 'bank' differs in 'river bank' vs 'bank account.' Sentence embeddings extend this to whole sentences, enabling semantic similarity search, clustering, and retrieval by comparing vectors with cosine similarity, the backbone of modern semantic search and RAG systems. You apply embeddings practically: powering text classification, clustering documents by meaning, and finding similar texts. This embedding-centric view is the bridge from classical NLP to today's transformer-based systems.
days.practical_task: Generate word and sentence embeddings for a text corpus, use cosine similarity to find semantically similar items, and train a classifier on sentence embeddings, comparing to a TF-IDF baseline.
days.mini_project: Build a semantic search or document-clustering system using sentence embeddings that lets you query by meaning, evaluate its retrieval quality, and compare it to a keyword-based approach.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between static (Word2Vec) and contextual (BERT) embeddings with an example word whose meaning depends on context.

days.title: Transformers and Hugging Face
objectives: Use pretrained transformers via Hugging Face
Fine-tune a transformer for a task
days.intermediate_explanation: Transformers power modern AI, from BERT to GPT, and understanding them is now core to data science. Their breakthrough is the self-attention mechanism: instead of processing a sequence step by step like an RNN, attention lets every token directly look at every other token and weigh their relevance, capturing long-range dependencies and, critically, doing so in parallel, which makes training on massive data feasible. Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel to capture different relationship types. The architecture comes in flavors: encoder-only (BERT) for understanding tasks like classification, decoder-only (GPT) for generation, and encoder-decoder for translation/summarization. The dominant paradigm is pretraining then fine-tuning: models are pretrained on enormous unlabeled corpora to learn language broadly, then fine-tuned on your specific labeled task with comparatively little data, delivering state-of-the-art results. The Hugging Face ecosystem makes all of this accessible: the transformers library provides thousands of pretrained models with a uniform API, datasets handles data efficiently, and a few lines let you load, fine-tune, and deploy. Learning to wield transformers via Hugging Face lets you solve advanced NLP tasks and sets the stage for working with large language models.

days.title: Large Language Models for Data Science
objectives: Use LLMs effectively in data workflows
Apply prompt engineering and structured output
topics: How LLMs work and their capabilities/limits
LLMs for data tasks (extraction, labeling, analysis)
days.intermediate_explanation: Large Language Models have become general-purpose tools that data scientists use both to build features/products and to accelerate their own work. Conceptually, an LLM is a very large transformer trained to predict the next token, which, at scale, yields surprising abilities to reason, summarize, extract, and generate, but with real limits: it can hallucinate (produce confident falsehoods), has a knowledge cutoff, and is nondeterministic. Prompt engineering is the practical craft of getting reliable outputs: clear instructions, few-shot examples that demonstrate the desired pattern, and techniques like chain-of-thought for reasoning tasks. For programmatic use you need structured output, prompting the model to return JSON, or using function/tool calling, so results are machine-parseable and integrate into pipelines. The most important production pattern is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): you embed your documents into a vector store, retrieve the most relevant chunks for a query using semantic similarity, and feed them to the LLM as context, grounding its answers in your data and reducing hallucination. Data scientists also use LLMs directly for practical tasks, extracting structured data from unstructured text, labeling data at scale, and drafting analysis, dramatically boosting productivity. Understanding both the power and the pitfalls lets you use LLMs responsibly and effectively.

days.title: MLOps: Experiment Tracking and Reproducibility
objectives: Make ML work reproducible and comparable
days.intermediate_explanation: As you run dozens of experiments, ad-hoc notebooks make it impossible to remember which configuration produced which result, and MLOps practices bring order and reproducibility. Experiment tracking is the first pillar: tools like MLflow automatically log each run's parameters (hyperparameters, data version), metrics (accuracy, loss over time), and artifacts (the trained model, plots, confusion matrices), giving you a searchable, comparable history so you can answer 'what was my best model and how did I get it?' months later. This transforms guesswork into systematic, comparable science. A model registry versions your models and manages their lifecycle stages (staging, production, archived), so you always know which model is deployed and can roll back. Because ML depends on data as much as code, you also version data and pipelines, DVC (Data Version Control) tracks large datasets and model files alongside Git, linking a code commit to the exact data and model it produced, achieving true end-to-end reproducibility. Together these practices solve the reproducibility crisis that plagues ML: without them, results can't be trusted or rebuilt. Adopting experiment tracking and versioning is a defining step from doing ad-hoc data science to doing production-grade, accountable ML.

days.title: Model Deployment with FastAPI and Docker
objectives: Serve models via a REST API
topics: Building a prediction API with FastAPI
days.intermediate_explanation: To deliver value, a model must be accessible to other software, and the standard way is to serve it behind a REST API packaged in a container. First you serialize the trained model (joblib, pickle, or a framework format) so it can be loaded without retraining. FastAPI is the modern Python framework for this: it's fast, async-capable, and auto-generates interactive API docs; you define an endpoint that receives input, runs the model, and returns predictions as JSON. Crucially, you validate incoming requests with Pydantic models, which enforce types and constraints, so malformed input is rejected cleanly rather than crashing your service, an essential production safeguard. Then you containerize with Docker: a Dockerfile specifies the exact environment (Python version, dependencies, model files) so your service runs identically on your laptop, a teammate's machine, or a cloud server, eliminating 'works on my machine' problems and making deployment portable and reproducible. You also learn to test the API and consider basic performance (batching, async, keeping the model loaded in memory). This deployment skillset is what lets a data scientist ship models as real services that applications and users can call, a capability that dramatically increases your impact and employability.

days.title: ML Pipelines and Orchestration
days.intermediate_explanation: Real ML systems are not single scripts but multi-step workflows, ingest data, validate it, engineer features, train, evaluate, and deploy, that must run reliably on a schedule, and orchestration tools manage this. The central abstraction is the DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph): you define tasks and their dependencies, and the orchestrator runs them in the correct order, in parallel where possible. Apache Airflow is the most widely adopted (with modern alternatives like Prefect and Dagster offering nicer developer experiences): you author pipelines as code, schedule them (e.g., retrain nightly, score hourly), and the tool handles execution. Production-grade features are what make orchestration essential: automatic retries when a task fails transiently, alerting when something breaks, backfilling historical runs, and a UI to monitor status and debug failures, none of which a bare cron job provides well. You use this to automate the recurring work of ML: scheduled retraining as new data arrives, and batch inference jobs that score large datasets on a cadence. Learning orchestration lets a data scientist build dependable, self-running pipelines instead of manually re-running notebooks, which is fundamental to operating ML in production.

days.title: Model Monitoring and Drift Detection
objectives: Set up alerting and retraining triggers
days.intermediate_explanation: A model's job doesn't end at deployment, in fact, that's when the risk begins, because models silently degrade over time and monitoring is how you catch it before it causes harm. The reason is that the world changes: the data a model sees in production drifts away from what it was trained on. You distinguish data drift (the distribution of inputs changes, e.g., a new customer demographic) from concept drift (the relationship between inputs and the target changes, e.g., buying behavior shifts after an economic change), each requiring different responses. Effective monitoring tracks several layers: operational metrics (latency, errors), input data health (missing values, distribution shifts, out-of-range values), prediction distributions, and, when ground truth eventually arrives, actual model performance. Statistical tests and tools like Evidently detect drift by comparing production data to a reference window and flagging significant divergence. You then wire up alerting so the team is notified, and, ideally, automated retraining triggers that refresh the model when drift or performance decay crosses a threshold, closing the loop. This monitoring discipline is what makes deployed ML trustworthy and durable rather than a fire-and-forget liability, and it's a core responsibility of production data science.

days.intermediate_explanation: When data grows beyond what a single machine can hold, you need distributed computing, and Apache Spark is the industry standard. Spark spreads data across a cluster of machines and executes computations in parallel, letting you process terabytes with the same logical operations you use on a Pandas DataFrame. The key mental model is lazy evaluation: transformations (filter, select, join, groupBy) build up a plan but nothing runs until an action (count, collect, write) triggers it, which lets Spark's Catalyst optimizer rewrite and optimize the whole computation before executing. The DataFrame API is the modern, optimized interface (RDDs are the lower-level foundation you rarely touch directly), and Spark SQL lets you query distributed data with plain SQL. Performance in Spark is dominated by shuffles - operations like joins and groupBys that move data across the network - so understanding partitioning and how to minimize shuffles is what separates a working Spark job from a fast one. Even if you use managed platforms like Databricks, these fundamentals let you reason about why a job is slow and how to fix it, which is essential when working at real-world data scale.

days.intermediate_explanation: Almost all production data science runs in the cloud, so knowing how to use it is a core practitioner skill. The foundation is cheap, durable object storage (S3 on AWS, GCS on Google Cloud) which holds the raw and processed data that forms a data lake. On top of that, managed compute lets you spin up powerful machines or GPU instances on demand and shut them down when done, so you pay only for what you use. Managed ML platforms like SageMaker and Vertex AI provide hosted notebooks, training jobs, and model endpoints so you do not maintain servers yourself, while cloud data warehouses like BigQuery, Redshift, and Snowflake let you run SQL over massive datasets with separated storage and compute that scales automatically. Underpinning all of this are infrastructure concepts you must respect: IAM controls who can access what (getting this wrong causes both outages and breaches), containers make your environment portable, and autoscaling adjusts capacity to load. Two things bite newcomers hardest - cost (a forgotten GPU cluster or an unpartitioned warehouse query can cost thousands) and security (leaked credentials or public buckets). Understanding these lets you build data science systems that scale without surprising bills or breaches.
