days.intermediate_explanation: At the intermediate level, the analysis is only half the job - the other half is getting people to understand and act on it. Data storytelling is the discipline of turning results into a clear narrative aimed at a specific audience. That starts with visualization principles: choose the chart that matches the message (trends over time as lines, comparisons as bars, relationships as scatter), maximize the data-to-ink ratio, use color deliberately, and never distort scales in ways that mislead. Advanced tools like Plotly and Altair let you add interactivity so stakeholders can explore, but the goal is always clarity, not decoration. Beyond charts, structure matters: lead with the conclusion (executives read top-down), support it with evidence, and always translate statistical findings into business terms - not 'the coefficient was 0.3 with p<0.01' but 'each extra onboarding email increased retention by an estimated 3%, worth roughly $X per year.' Quantifying impact and tailoring depth to the audience (technical appendix for peers, one-slide summary for leadership) is what turns a correct analysis into an influential one. Many strong models never ship because the analyst could not communicate their value; mastering this makes your work matter.

days.title: Responsible AI, Fairness, and Governance
objectives: Identify and mitigate bias in data and models

days.intermediate_explanation: As your models influence real decisions - loans, hiring, healthcare, pricing - the responsibility to make them fair, private, and accountable becomes part of the job, not an afterthought. Bias enters through data (historical discrimination baked into labels, under-represented groups, biased sampling) and through modeling choices, and it can cause real harm and legal liability. Fairness metrics let you measure it: demographic parity checks whether positive predictions are equal across groups, while equalized odds checks whether error rates are equal - and crucially, these definitions can conflict, so you must choose the one that fits the context. Mitigation happens at three stages: pre-processing the data (reweighting, resampling), in-processing during training (fairness constraints), and post-processing the predictions (adjusting thresholds per group). Privacy is the other pillar: you must handle personally identifiable information carefully, anonymize where possible, and understand techniques like differential privacy that add mathematical noise to protect individuals. Finally, governance ties it together - documenting models (model cards, datasheets), maintaining auditability, and complying with regulations like GDPR and the EU AI Act. A data scientist who ignores these ships models that get pulled, sued, or cause harm; one who masters them builds trustworthy systems that survive scrutiny.

days.assignment: Explain a real-world case where a deployed model caused biased or harmful outcomes, identify where bias entered, and describe how fairness metrics and governance could have caught it.

days.title: Capstone Part 1: Problem Framing and Data Foundation
objectives: Frame a real business problem as a data science project
Build a reproducible data and feature pipeline
topics: Translating a business problem into ML objectives and metrics
Building a reproducible ingestion and cleaning pipeline
Feature engineering and a versioned dataset
days.intermediate_explanation: The capstone is where every skill from the previous six weeks comes together on one substantial, portfolio-worthy project, and Part 1 is about doing the unglamorous but decisive work of framing and foundations. First you translate a real business problem ('reduce customer churn', 'forecast demand', 'detect fraud') into a precise ML formulation: what exactly are you predicting, what is the target, and - critically - what metric defines success in business terms, not just accuracy. You set a baseline (a trivial model or current process) so improvements are measurable, and you define success criteria up front to avoid moving goalposts. Then you build the data foundation properly: a reproducible pipeline that ingests, cleans, and validates data, engineered features stored as a versioned dataset, and thorough EDA to understand distributions, leakage risks, and data quality issues before any modeling. Getting this stage right is what separates a project that produces trustworthy results from one that silently learns from leaked or broken data. By the end of Part 1 you have a clearly framed problem, a clean versioned dataset, a baseline, and a validation strategy - the platform everything else is built on.

days.assignment: Write and defend your success metric and baseline choice, explaining why they reflect real business value and how you will guard against data leakage.

days.title: Capstone Part 2: Modeling, Optimization, and Validation
objectives: Build, tune, and rigorously validate models for the capstone
Compare approaches and interpret the best model
topics: Iterative modeling from baseline to advanced models
days.intermediate_explanation: With a solid foundation from Part 1, Part 2 is the modeling heart of the capstone, and the emphasis is on rigor, not just fitting an algorithm. You work iteratively: start from your baseline, try progressively stronger models (regularized linear models, gradient boosting, and where appropriate deep learning), and improve features based on what you learn. Every experiment is tracked with a tool like MLflow so results are reproducible and comparable rather than lost in notebook cells. Validation strategy is critical and must match the data - stratified cross-validation for classification, time-series splits for temporal data, and nested CV when you also tune hyperparameters, so your reported performance is an honest estimate and not the result of leakage or overfitting to the validation set. Hyperparameter optimization (with Optuna) squeezes out real gains, but you select the final model on the validation strategy and then confirm on a held-out test set exactly once. Finally you interpret the chosen model with SHAP and do error analysis - where does it fail, on which segments, and why - because understanding failures is what makes the model trustworthy and points to the next improvement. By the end you have a validated, tuned, well-understood model with a documented experiment trail.
days.practical_task: For your capstone dataset, run a tracked modeling workflow: baseline, at least two advanced models with tuned hyperparameters using an appropriate CV strategy, then select and interpret the best model with SHAP.

days.assignment: Document your cross-validation strategy and justify it for your data type, then explain one failure mode you found in error analysis and how you would address it.

days.title: Capstone Part 3: Deployment, Communication, and Career Launch
objectives: Deploy and monitor the capstone model
Communicate results and build a strong portfolio
Prepare for intermediate/senior data science roles
topics: Packaging and deploying the model (FastAPI + Docker)
Basic monitoring and a retraining plan
Writing up results and an executive summary
days.intermediate_explanation: Part 3 turns your validated model into something real and something that advances your career. First you deploy it: package the model behind a FastAPI service, containerize it with Docker so it runs identically anywhere, and expose a clean prediction endpoint - closing the loop from data to a usable product. You add basic monitoring (logging predictions, tracking input drift, defining when to retrain) so the deployed model does not silently rot, demonstrating that you understand ML as a lifecycle, not a one-off. Then you communicate: write up the project as a clear case study with the problem, approach, results in business terms, and honest limitations, plus an executive summary. This becomes a centerpiece of your portfolio - a well-documented GitHub repository and a presentable narrative that shows not just that you can model, but that you can deliver end-to-end. Finally you prepare for the job market at the intermediate/senior level: structuring your portfolio around impact, practicing how to explain your capstone's technical and business decisions, and readying yourself for interviews that probe statistics, ML, coding, and system design. Finishing this roadmap you have both a deployed, monitored, well-communicated project and the professional positioning to move into more advanced data science roles.
